coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of green nanosorbent from bovine serum albumin and curcumin for magnetic solid phase extraction of pesticides from food samples

Nojavan, Saeed
coredata.dc:description: For the first time, a magnetic carbon nanocomposite was synthesized using one-step hydrothermal procedure, employing bovine serum albumin, curcumin, and ferric ammonium citrate. Additionally, the application of this novel composite as an adsorbent for magnetic dispersive solid phase extraction of fungicides and pesticides from water and food samples is a unique aspect of this study. Under optimum conditions (salt concentration: 5.0% w/v, pH: 7.0, desorption solvent: ethanol, sorbent amount: 20 mg, extraction time: 20 min, desorption time: 3 min, stirring rate: 500 rpm, sample volume: 30 mL, extraction temperature: room temperature, and desorption solvent volume: 150 μL) linearity (2.5 to 1400 ng mL−1), coefficients of determination (R2 ≥ 0.997), limits of detection (0.75 to 1.5 ng mL−1), and limits of quantification (2.5 to 5.0 ng mL−1) were achieved. The method validation results showed extraction recovery ranging from 71.2% to 93.4%, and preconcentration factors ranging from 142.5 to 186.1.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of green nanosorbent from bovine serum albumin and curcumin for magnetic solid phase extraction of pesticides from food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry and Pollutants, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Analytical Chemistry and Pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry and Pollutants, Shahid Beheshti University, Evin, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the first time, a magnetic carbon nanocomposite was synthesized using one-step hydrothermal procedure, employing bovine serum albumin, curcumin, and ferric ammonium citrate. Additionally, the application of this novel composite as an adsorbent for magnetic dispersive solid phase extraction of fungicides and pesticides from water and food samples is a unique aspect of this study. Under optimum conditions (salt concentration: 5.0% w/v, pH: 7.0, desorption solvent: ethanol, sorbent amount: 20 mg, extraction time: 20 min, desorption time: 3 min, stirring rate: 500 rpm, sample volume: 30 mL, extraction temperature: room temperature, and desorption solvent volume: 150 μL) linearity (2.5 to 1400 ng mL−1), coefficients of determination (R2 ≥ 0.997), limits of detection (0.75 to 1.5 ng mL−1), and limits of quantification (2.5 to 5.0 ng mL−1) were achieved. The method validation results showed extraction recovery ranging from 71.2% to 93.4%, and preconcentration factors ranging from 142.5 to 186.1.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Farmers frequently use pesticides and fungicides, such as organophosphorus pesticides (OPPs) and triazole fungicides (TFs), in agriculture to control and prevent pests and diseases to achieve high-quality agricultural products. Nevertheless, these substances have the ability to accumulate in planet tissues, soil and water, potentially harming ecosystems and entering the food chain (Fu et al., 2023). Thus, maximum residue limits (MRLs) for pesticide and fungicide residues in food products have been established by regulatory agencies worldwide. These limits are based on the maximum amount of residues that can be present in food products without posing a risk to human health (Hejazi, Grant, & Peterson, 2022). Detecting and monitoring residues of pesticides and fungicides in food products and environmental samples can be a challenging task. Hence, it is crucial to develop simple, fast, and sensitive techniques for continuous measurement to accurately detect OPPs and TFs in various samples. Given that pesticides are often present in agricultural products at trace levels and complex matrices, sample preparation is necessary to achieve precise analysis. Therefore, sample preparation plays a vital role in eliminating interfering compounds and achieving the preconcentration of analytes (Liu et al., 2022). Numerous sample preparation techniques have been introduced to extract and preconcentrate pesticides, with solvents serving as the basis for many of these methods. Some of these techniques include homogeneous liquid–liquid extraction (Wang et al., 2008), dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction (Moinfar & Hosseini, 2009), and cloud point extraction (Zhao et al., 2011). Additional sample pretreatment methods have been implemented by utilizing an adsorbent, which includes solid-phase extraction (Amendola, Pelosi, & Dommarco, 2010), solid-phase microextraction (Zang et al., 2023), dispersive solid-phase extraction (Mao et al., 2021), magnetic dispersive solid-phase extraction (MDSPE) (Jiang, Li, Cui, Wang, & Zhao, 2019), and stir bar sorptive extraction (He et al., 2021). Adsorbent-based extraction has become increasingly popular and well-established in comparison with solvent-based extraction methods due to its utilization of less toxic solvents, ease of modification, and ability to be employed in various formats such as cartridges, disks, or microplates (Aladaghlo, Javanbakht, Sahragard, Fakhari, & Shaabani, 2023; Behpour, Shadi, & Nojavan, 2023; Huang et al., 2022). Different adsorbents can be selected based on their specific chemical properties and affinities, enabling the targeted extraction of analytes with varying polarities, sizes, and functional groups. This adaptability makes adsorbent-based extraction applicable to a wide range of analytes across diverse sample matrices. Among all the adsorbent-based extraction techniques, MDSPE can be considered the most efficient method due to its ability to quickly separate, ease of operation, and time-saving characteristics (Majd & Nojavan, 2021). Magnetic carbon composite (MCC), which is synthesized through the hydrothermal method, can be utilized as an adsorbent for the extraction and preconcentration of pesticides (Ren, Zhang, Cao, Wang, & Zhou, 2018). To synthesize MCC through hydrothermal methods, a mixture of carbon precursor, reductant and iron salt is heated within a sealed vessel under high pressure and temperature conditions (Wu et al., 2020). This process leads to the creation of a composite material that possesses both magnetic and carbon properties. Compared to alternative methods such as incorporating magnetic components onto the carbonaceous substrate after growth or using pre-synthesized magnetic components in carbonaceous precursors, this method has a clear advantage in terms of providing a more straightforward route to achieving the final product (Kermani, Jafari, & Saraji, 2019). In general, MCCs have demonstrated potential as an adsorbent of a wide range of pollutants from environmental samples. The selectivity and sensitivity of MCCs can be enhanced by selecting appropriate carbon precursors and functionalization methods. The development of new MCCs and analytical methods is an active area of research in this field, with potential applications in various fields.

This study aims to synthesize a green magnetic carbon nanocomposite using a straightforward one-step hydrothermal process and investigate its ability as an effective and novel sorbent in magnetic SPE for the extraction of OPPs (fenitrothion (FEN) and chlorpyrifos (CPS)) as well as TFs (triticonazole (TRZ), diniconazole (DIN), and tebuconazole (TEB)) from agricultural water and food samples prior to GC-FID analysis. Since the fabricated sorbent has many functional groups on the surface, it is a good candidate for adsorption of pesticides and fungicides. To the best of our knowledge, this study represents the first attempt on using ferric ammonium citrate (FAC), bovine serum albumin (BSA), and curcumin (CCM) as raw materials for the synthesis of a magnetic carbon nanocomposite using a simple one-step hydrothermal method.

The compounds including FEN (MW = 277.24 g/mol, Log P = 3.29, pka = not available), CPS (MW = 350.59 g/mol, Log P = 4.96, pka = 4.6), TRZ (MW = 317.80 g/mol, Log P = 3.29, pka = 13), DIN (MW = 326.20 g/mol, Log P = 4.3, pka = 12.89), and TEB (MW = 307.82 g/mol, Log P = 3.7, pka = 13.7) were kindly gifts from Ariashimi Company (Tehran, Iran). The structures of these analytes are depicted in Fig. S1 (see electronic supplementary information (ESM)). BSA (BSA amount ≥ 98%), CCM, and FAC were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Fluorene (Fluo) was purchased from the Chem Service Inc. (West Chester, USA). Methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile (ACN), and acetone were obtained from Dr. Mojallali (Tehran, Iran). Hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The Millipore system (Millipore, Milford, MA, USA) was utilized to prepare ultrapure water.

To prepare a stock standard solution with a concentration of 1000 mg L−1 for each target analyte, an adequate amount of each analyte was dissolved in ethanol. To avoid the decomposition of the analytes, the standard solutions were kept in a dark and cool environment at a temperature of 4 °C in a refrigerator. To maintain consistency and accuracy, a mixed standard solution containing the target compounds with a concentration of 100 mg L−1 was prepared in ethanol every week. The freshly prepared solution was then stored at 4 °C to preserve its integrity. To perform extraction procedure, working solutions with different concentrations were prepared daily by diluting the standard solution with deionized water. A stock solution of Fluo (1000 mg L−1) was prepared in ethanol, and it was diluted to 150 mg L−1 every week before being used as an internal standard.

Agricultural wastewater sample was obtained from a garden in Sabzevar, Iran. The water sample was initially filtered using 0.22 μm filters, followed by pH adjustment using HCl/NaOH solutions (0.1 M) via a pH meter. Subsequent extraction was carried out without any further dilution. The fresh carrot, apple, and potato samples randomly achieved from different local supermarket in Tehran, Iran. Each fruit and vegetable sample was sliced into small pieces and homogenized using a high-speed homogenizer. Then, to begin the analysis, precisely 10 g of each uniform solid sample was placed into a 50-mL centrifuge tube and spiked with specific quantities of analytes from a mixed standard solution. Next, 10 mL of ACN was added to the centrifuge tube and ultrasonicated for 30 min. To eliminate the pulps from the samples, they were centrifuged for 3 min at 6000 rpm. Finally, the supernatant obtained was dried using a rotary vacuum evaporator, and then re-dissolved with 30 mL HPLC grade water in a centrifuge tube(Behpour et al., 2023). Afterward, the pH of the sample was adjusted using the same diluted HCl/NaOH solutions as mentioned above. Subsequently, the extraction process was performed.

In this study, a Varian CP-3800 GC-FID system (Palo Alto, CA, USA) with a fused silica capillary column (HP-5, 30 m × 0.32 mm × 0.25 mm film thickness, Agilent, USA) was used to monitor and measure the analytes. The temperature of the column oven was programmed as follows: it was initially set to 60 °C and held for 1 min, then increased to 160 °C at a rate of 20 °C/min and held for 1 min, followed by a ramp to 220 °C at a rate of 30 °C /min and held for 1 min, and finally raised to 280 °C at a rate of 30 °C/min and held for 2 min. The FID and Injection port temperature were set to 300 °C and 280 °C, respectively. The GC instrument was equipped with a split-splitless injector, and the split ratio was adjusted to 5. A constant flow rate of 1.9 mL/min of nitrogen gas (99.999%) was used as the carrier gas. Heidolph MR 3001 K heater stirrer (Schwabach, Germany) was used for the synthesis process. IKA 2572100 RW 16 Overhead Stirrer (Cole-Parmer United Kingdom) was utilized for the extraction process. The real samples were centrifuged using a GHB Fixette II centrifuge (Wehingen, Germany). The desorption step was carried out utilizing an ultrasonic device (Elma, Germany). The FTIR spectra of various adsorbents in the range of 400–4000 cm− 1 (KBr pellet) were recorded by a Thermo Nicolet NEXUS 470 FTIR instrument (Waltham, Massachusetts, USA). To study the X-ray patterns, the X'Pert Pro model diffractometer instrument (PANalytical, Almelo, The Netherlands), which utilized a Cu K-alpha radiation source, was employed. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were investigated using a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi Mg X-ray source (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., U.S.A.). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) (MIRA3TESCAN-XMU, Brno, Czech Republic) containing an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was applied to investigate the surface morphology and identify elemental composition of sample. The magnetic behavior of the materials was recorded by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) device (LBKFB, Meghnatis Kavir Kashan Co, Iran). The pH of solutions was adjusted by a pH-meter (model 827, Metrohm, Germany). The weight loss of the sample was studied using a BAHR STA 503 thermogravimetric analyzer instrument. The sample, weighing approximately 5.0 mg, was heated in a nitrogen atmosphere from room temperature up to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The particle size of the adsorbent was determined using a dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern Zetasizer, ZEN3600, UK).

Initially, 1.0 g of FAC as a magnetic precursor (Fe3O4) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 0.5 g of BSA and 0.5 g of CCM to the solution. After stirring for 20 min, the resulting dispersion was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 20 mL and heated at 200 °C for 6 h. The autoclave was allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. Finally, the obtained product, labeled Fe3O4@BC, was isolated from the liquid phase by using a magnet. The acquired powder was washed by distilled water (3 times, 20 mL) and ethanol (4 times, 20 mL) and then dried at 50 °C in an air-circulating oven for 5 h. For the comparison purpose, the above synthesis method was performed without using CCM and the obtained product was named Fe3O4@B. The process of synthesis and application of Fe3O4@BC is illustrated in the Fig. 1
.

To a centrifuge tube containing 30 mL of the sample solution with the target analytes and 1.5 g of NaCl (as a salting-out reagent), 20 mg of the magnetic carbon nanocomposite was added. In order to enhance the dispersion of the sorbent and facilitate the extraction process, the mixture was subjected to 30 s of sonication. While sonication excels at dispersing particles, it can lead to heating during extended extraction. This heating can negatively impact the reproducibility of results across experiments. To ensure consistent extraction and avoid this issue, the sample solution was stirred using an overhead stirrer at 500 rpm for a duration of 20 min. Upon completion of the extraction process, an external magnet was used to isolate the sorbent from the solution, and the supernatant was then decanted. In the desorption step, 150 μL of ethanol was utilized to desorb the analytes from the nanoadsorbent under sonication for 3 min. Next, 10 μL of the internal standard solution (Fluo) was added to 50 μL of the desorption solvent. The final step involved manually injecting 1.0 μL of this solution into the GC-FID instrument, and the ratio of the analyte response to the internal standard response was calculated and used to perform the analysis of the target analytes. The internal standard is used in GC to account for variations in injection volume and other potential errors during analysis, leading to more accurate quantification.

Magnetic precursor, reductant and carbon source are the raw materials used to synthesize most MCC through hydrothermal method. In this regard, FAC was selected as the magnetic precursor due to its low toxicity, affordability, and high stability (Wang et al., 2017). Furthermore, FAC possesses inherent reducing properties, which can aid in the reduction of metal ions during the synthesis process. This capability allows for the in situ formation of magnetic nanoparticles from iron ions, which can then be embedded within the carbon structure (Teja & Koh, 2009). BSA can be used as a reducing agent to convert metal ions into magnetic nanoparticles, which can then be incorporated into the carbon matrix to produce MCCs. During hydrothermal treatment, deamination of BSA can form ammonia, which can adjust the pH of the solution and promote the formation of magnetic particles (Sheehan & Savage, 2017). CCM, as a carbon source, contains functional groups such as phenolic and keto groups that exhibit high reactivity under hydrothermal conditions (Pal, Mohiyuddin, & Packirisamy, 2018; Ting et al., 2018). Additionally, CCM provides more functional groups on the surface of the adsorbent. These functional groups play a crucial role in enhancing the interactions between the adsorbent and analytes, ultimately leading to improved extraction efficiency.

FTIR spectroscopy was employed to identify the surface functional groups of the prepared magnetic carbon nanocomposite. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4@B is depicted in Fig. S2 (see ESM), which shows that the peak at 3200–3400 cm−1 corresponds to OH and NH stretching vibrations. The absorption signal observed at 1632 cm−1 in the vibrational spectrum may be ascribed to the vibration of CC and CO. The presence of FeO can be associated with the band detected at 580 cm−1. These observations indicate that BSA was consumed in the hydrothermal processing to produce Fe3O4@B. Fig. S2 (see ESM) also displays the FT-IR spectra of the Fe3O4@BC. The stretching vibrations of CN, and COO− can be associated with the band detected at 1513 cm−1. Absorption bands at 1270 and 1050 cm− 1 are attributed to the C-O-C and CO, respectively. In comparison with Fe3O4@B, these new bands suggested that CCM was also consumed in the hydrothermal processing to create surface functional groups and adhere to the Fe3O4 particles.

Fig. 2A displays the FE-SEM image of the sample. The SEM image depicts an irregular morphology of the magnetic carbon nanocomposite, with an average size of approximately 20 nm. The SEM image clearly showed the agglomeration of the magnetic carbon nanocomposite, which was attributed to its magnetic attraction (Wu et al., 2020). The elemental composition of Fe3O4@BC was analyzed using EDS for both qualitative and quantitative purposes. The elemental composition analysis, presented in Fig. 2B, revealed that Fe3O4@BC was composed of carbon (37.74%wt), oxygen (25.70%wt), nitrogen (0.86%wt), and iron (35.70%wt).

XPS was also utilized to probe the surface composition of the magnetic carbon nanocomposite. The XPS full-scan spectrum displayed in Fig. 3A confirms the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and iron. The high-resolution deconvoluted C 1 s spectra presented four peaks at 284.6, 286.4, 288.3 and 289.0 eV, which referred to the C-C/C=C, C-OH/C-NH/C-O-C, C=O/C=N and COOH, respectively, as seen in Fig. 3B. For N 1 s (Fig. 3C), three fitting peaks at 399.5, 400.4, and 401.3 eV can be assigned to the NH bond of amine groups, pyrrolic N and graphite N, respectively. As seen in Fig. 3D, the O 1 s band was deconvoluted into FeO (529.6 eV), Fe-O-C (531.6 eV), CO (532.6 eV), C-OH/C-O-C (533.2 eV), and COOH (534.4 eV). The results of the three mentioned techniques demonstrate a mutual confirmation, indicating a consistent agreement among the findings.

The TGA of magnetic carbon nanocomposite was shown in Fig. S3. The result of TGA demonstrated the slight weight loss at 50–180 °C which attributed to the evaporation of water and the loss of gas molecules on the sample surface (Bayatloo & Nojavan, 2022). The sharp weight loss at 270 to 450 °C was probably due to the decomposition of the carbon matrix and functional groups, which was indicated the mass loss to be 52 wt%. After 450 °C, the residual mass was related to the Fe3O4 NPs, which was about 43%.

VSM tests were carried out at room temperature to investigate the magnetic properties of both Fe3O4@B and Fe3O4@BC. As can be seen in Fig. S4, the maximal saturation magnetizations of Fe3O4@B, and Fe3O4@BC were 50, and 15 emu g− 1, respectively. The process of hydrothermal treatment consumes CCM, resulting in the formation of surface functional groups and its adhesion to Fe3O4 particles. This leads to a decrease in saturation magnetization due to the shielding effect. Despite the decrease in magnetization values, Fe3O4@BC can still be conveniently separated from a solution using a magnet and used for MDSPE purposes.

Fig. S5 demonstrates XRD pattern of magnetic carbon nanocomposite. A broad peak with low intensity in the region of 10°-28° was attributed to the carbonaceous matrix (Ren et al., 2018). Furthermore, diffraction peaks at 2θ of 30.3°, 35.5°, 43.3°, 53.2°, 57.2°, and 62.8° were assigned to Fe3O4. Carbonaceous matrix has amorphous structure that causes some noises in the XRD pattern of magnetic carbon nanocomposite in comparison with commercial Fe3O4 (Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, Salavati-Niasari, & Ghanbari, 2018).

Fig. S6 displays the result of dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis, indicating that the Fe3O4@BC nanoparticles exhibit an average particle size of around 86 nm. The size distributions observed in DLS measurements are broader than those observed in FESEM, possibly due to minor aggregation occurring in the aqueous solution.

To optimize the procedure of MDSPE, several variables were examined individually to evaluate their impact. These variables included the type of sorbent, concentration of NaCl, pH of the sample solution, type of desorption solvent, adsorbent amount, extraction and desorption time, stirring rate, sample volume, extraction temperature, and desorption solvent volume. The solutions containing 250.0 ng mL−1 of each target analytes was used for optimization experiments. The initial extraction conditions included: salt concentration: 0.0% w/v, sample pH value: 7.0, desorption solvent type: ethanol, amount of sorbent: 20 mg, extraction time: 20 min, desorption time: 5 min, stirring rate: 500 rpm, sample volume:30 mL, extraction temperature: room temperature, and desorption solvent volume: 200 μL.

The extraction capability of Fe3O4@B and Fe3O4@BC was evaluated, and the results are depicted in Fig. S7. Based on the results, Fe3O4@BC showed extraction recovery about 38–77%, while Fe3O4@B showed extraction recovery about 20–42%. Compared to Fe3O4@B, the Fe3O4@BC exhibited a significant enhancement in extraction efficiency. This improvement can be attributed to the increase in functional groups on the surface of the adsorbent resulting from the consumption of CCM during the hydrothermal process. Generally, increasing the number of functional groups enhances the active sites on the surface of the adsorbent, thereby improving the extraction recovery.

Salt concentration may indicate significant effects on partitioning of target analytes between the sample solution and adsorbent. In general, the addition of salt can enhance the recovery of extraction by reducing the solubility of organic analytes in aqueous solutions, attributable to the salting-out effect, and altering the distribution constant of analytes between the adsorbent and the aqueous solution (Gao, Liu, Sun, Zhao, & He, 2020). Furthermore, the viscosity of sample solution can also increase at higher salt concertation that will reduce the mass transfer of target analytes to the adsorbent surface, and eventually decreases the extraction yield. To investigate the influence of salt concentrations on the extraction performance, NaCl was added in the range of 0.0–15.0% w/v to the sample solution. As illustrated in Fig. 4A, extraction yield for the target analytes enhanced as the concentration of NaCl increased from 0.0 to 5.0% w/v. However, extraction recovery decreased when the NaCl content increased to 15.0% w/v. This reduction is attributed to the increased viscosity of the sample solution at higher salt concentrations, which diminishes the mass transfer of target analytes to the adsorbent surface, consequently lowering the extraction yield (Yazdanpanah & Nojavan, 2021). The results were consistent with as discussed above. Considering the results, 5.0% w/v NaCl was applied as optimal salt concentration in further experiments.

The pH of sample solution is an important factor that influences the sorbent's surface charge and the ionic/neutral form of the analytes (Wang et al., 2018). The pH of sample solution was adjusted within the range of 2.0 to 10.0. As shown in Fig. 4B, low signal was acquired in highly acidic and basic media, which may be due to degradation or ionized of TFs and OPPs in these conditions (Behpour et al., 2023; Ren et al., 2018). Furthermore, in acidic solutions, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of the sorbent, which are active sites for hydrogen bonding, can become protonated and potentially inaccessible. Additionally, target analytes tend to be positively charged in acidic conditions due to their pKa values (Nasiri, Ahmadzadeh, & Amiri, 2022; Su et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2022). Therefore, in acidic conditions, the repulsion between the positive charges on the sorbent and the target analytes leads to the weakest interactions between them. As the pH increases, deprotonation procedure happens and electrostatic repulsion decreases, and other interactions (π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding) between analytes and adsorbent can lead to enhance extraction efficiency. The results illustrate that the maximum extraction recovery was attained at the pH around 7.0. The enhanced extraction efficiency observed at pH 7.0 suggests that hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions between the analytes and the sorbent's surface are potential mechanisms for improving extraction recovery. Hence, pH 7.0 was selected as the optimum pH for the proposed method.

Elution of analytes from adsorbent has a crucial role for obtaining a proper extraction efficiency. To ensure effective elution, the solvent used should have a high affinity for the target analytes and be compatible with the chromatographic system. To choose a suitable desorption solvent, ethanol, methanol, acetonitrile and acetone were investigated as elution solvents. These solvents were chosen based on earlier research (Aladaghlo et al., 2023; Ayazi, Ekhteraei, Pashayi, & Ahmadian, 2022; Sun et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2018). The poor dispersion of Fe3O4@BC in acetone is the reason for the low desorption efficiency observed in this case. The inadequate dispersion prevents sufficient contact between the Fe3O4@BC particles and the surrounding solvent, leading to reduced desorption efficiency. When using these solvents, the resolution for GC separation of target analytes and the peak shape remained unchanged or unaffected, indicating that the choice of solvents did not introduce any detrimental effects. As shown in Fig. S8, ethanol exhibited the best desorption results. Thus, ethanol was opted as the eluent.

To investigate how the quantity of sorbent affects the extraction process, different weights of the adsorbent (10–30 mg) were added to the sample solution. As depicted in Fig. 4C increasing the amount of adsorbent up to 20 mg enhances extraction efficiency. However, it levels off at 25 mg and exhibits a slight decrease at 30 mg. The slight decrease in extraction recovery could be attributed to the possibility that some of the target analytes on the adsorbent were not completely eluted by the solvent. Generally, increasing the amount of adsorbent enhances the availability of active sites for the adsorption of target analytes. However, the desorption of these analytes required a higher eluent volume, which consequently led to a decrease in preconcentration factor. Hence, in order to attain acceptable extraction recoveries, 20 mg was used as the optimized amount.

Since the migration of the target analyte from the sample solution to the surface of sorbent to achieve equilibrium is a time-dependent process, a certain duration is necessary for the interaction between the analytes and the sorbent to reach sorption equilibrium (Bayatloo & Nojavan, 2022). Therefore, a range of 5–30 min was explored to determine the optimal extraction time. The data presented in Fig. 4D, demonstrates that the extraction efficiency was increased up to 20 min and then did not show any further improvements with longer extraction times. Thus, 20 min was chosen as the equilibrium time for later experiments. Additionally, the influence of desorption time was studied within a time interval of 1–5 min using a sonicate. The selection of this method for the desorption step was based on previous researches (Amiri, Tayebee, Abdar, & Sani, 2019; Sahebi, Pourmortazavi, Zandavar, & Mirsadeghi, 2019; Seebunrueng, Tamuang, Ruangchai, Sansuk, & Srijaranai, 2021). As depicted in Fig. S9, the extraction efficiency of all analytes gradually increased up to 3 min and then achieved an almost constant value. Hence, to save time and maximize extraction efficiency, a desorption time of 3 min was chosen as the optimal time.

Stirring speed significantly impacts extraction processes by influencing the mass transfer of target analytes to the sorbent. Higher stirring rates can enhance this transfer, potentially leading to increased extraction recovery. To investigate this effect, the sample solution was stirred at speeds ranging from 150 to 900 rpm (Fig. S10). As expected, extraction recovery increased with stirring rate up to 500 rpm. Interestingly, the recovery remained constant even at 750 rpm before decreasing at 900 rpm. This decrease is likely due to the formation of air bubbles hindering mass transfer, or possibly analytes desorbing from the sorbent at high speeds. Consequently, 500 rpm was chosen as the optimal stirring rate for further experiments.

The extraction recovery, preconcentration factor, and sensitivity of the method are impacted by the volume of the sample solution. Thus, to explore this factor, different sample volumes (10, 20, 30, 35, and 40 mL) were examined. As depicted in Fig. S11, extraction recovery remains constant up to 30 mL, beyond which it starts to decrease. This decline can be attributed to the increased thickness of the boundary layer and the time required for analytes to reach the adsorption sites. Consequently, to achieve a proper preconcentration factor and acceptable sensitivity, 30 mL was chosen as the breaking point in this experiment.

The extraction efficiency can be influenced by temperature variations. Generally, increasing the temperature can enhance the extraction efficiency by increasing the diffusion rate of analytes to the solid phase sorbent. Alternatively, in some cases, when the temperature rises to a certain level, the extraction efficiency may decrease, which can be attributed to either the exothermic nature of the adsorption process or a decreased distribution coefficient between the sorbent and the sample matrix. In this study, the effect of temperature was investigated from 25 to 65 °C. As indicated in Fig. S12, it was observed that raising the temperature has no significant effect on extraction recovery. Consequently, room temperature was selected for subsequent experiments. This choice eliminates the need for specialized heating or cooling equipment and may better simulate real-life conditions, where samples are typically stored and handled at ambient temperatures.

The extraction efficiency can be affected by the volume of ethanol. Therefore, the volume of ethanol was investigated in the range of 100–300 μL. As shown in Fig. S13, the highest extraction efficiency was obtained with 150 μL of ethanol. When the desorption volume is smaller than 150 μL, it is not enough to effectively desorption the analytes that are bound to the surface of the sorbent. Generally, solvent is unable to effectively reach and interact with the sorbent to break the binding between the analytes and the sorbent. Furthermore, an excessive volume of desorption solvent diluted the extracted analytes and led to a reduction in the preconcentration factor. Therefore, to achieve proper extraction efficiency and preconcentration factor, 150 μL of ethanol was employed as the optimum desorption solvent volume.

The efficiency of a magnetic sorbent can be evaluated through its reusability. The used sorbent was washed two times with 3 mL of ethanol and once with 3 mL of deionized water, each step involving ultrasonication for 3 min. This procedure ensured the elimination of any residual target analytes on the sorbent before the subsequent use. The results indicated that the extraction efficiency of adsorbent remained stable with no significant alterations (< 10%), even after the sorbent had been reused five times (Fig. S14). Hence, it can be concluded that Fe3O4@BC demonstrates good reusability and stability in the MDSPE procedure for a minimum of five cycles.

Under the optimal extraction conditions, various analytical parameters such as linearity, coefficients of determination (R2), limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), relative standard deviation (RSD%) expressed as repeatability, accuracy, extraction recovery (ER), preconcentration factor (PF), and maximum PF were evaluated. ER, PF, and PFmax were determined using eqs. (1), (2), and (3), respectively.

The variable ns,initial represents the total moles of analyte initially present in the aqueous solution, while the variable na,final represents the total moles of analyte ultimately collected in the desorption solvent. Va and Vs are the volume of the desorption solvent and the volume of aqueous solution, respectively. The final concentration of the analyte in the desorption solvent is referred to as Ca,final, while Cs,initial denotes the initial concentration of the analyte in the aqueous solution.

Calibration curves for each target analytes were achieved by plotting the ratio of the analyte response to the internal standard response versus concentration of target analytes. As can be seen from Table 1 , all the analytes exhibited good linearity with a satisfactory determination coefficient (R2 = 0.997). The LODs (S/N = 3) indicate the lowest concentration of the analyte that can be detected, whereas the LOQs (S/N = 10) indicate the minimum concentration of the analyte that can be reliably quantified by utilizing the suggested method. As exhibited in Table 1, the LODs were between 0.75 and 1.5 ng mL−1, and LOQs were in the range of 2.5–5.0 ng mL−1. To evaluate the intra-day and inter-day repeatability (RSD%) of the method, the extraction procedure at three concentration levels (25.0, 100.0 and 250.0 ng mL−1) for each analyte was carried out by three parallel experiments at one day and three consecutive days, respectively. RSDs were in the range of 4.3–7.3% for intraday and 5.3–8.9% for inter-day. ER, PF, and PFmax values were calculated according to eqs. (1), (2), and (3), respectively. The calculated PFs ranged from 142.5 to 186.1, corresponding to extraction recoveries ranging from 71.2% to 93.4%, while PFmax was 200. Furthermore, the adsorption capacity of the sorbent for each analyte was assessed using eq. (4), and the results are demonstrated in Table S1. This parameter was calculated using a 30 mL sample solution at a concentration of 150.0 mg L−1.

In order to assess the practical efficiency of the recommended sorbent for real sample analysis, several samples, including apple, carrot, potato and agriculture water were meticulously prepared according to the guidelines provided in section 2.3. Subsequently, these prepared samples were analyzed by GC-FID under optimal conditions, each of samples replicated three times. The final results are tabulated in Table 2 . Due to the extensive utilization of fungicides and pesticides in agricultural fields, trace amounts of target analytes may be detected in real samples. Based on the results, none of the target analytes were observed in the real samples. Besides, in order to evaluate the accuracy of the method and matrix effect, the real samples (apple, carrot, and potato) were spiked with 75.0 and 250.0 μg kg−1, and agricultural water was spiked at concentrations of 50.0 and 150.0 ng mL−1 of all five analytes. Finally, they were analyzed by the optimized method. The relative recoveries (RRs%) of the proposed method were determined by the following equation:
(5)
RR % =
C found
−
C real
C added
× 100

Where Cfound is used to denote the concentration of the analyte in the real sample after the addition of a specific amount of standard, Creal is the initial concentration of analyte in the real sample before spiking a certain amount of standard solution, while Cadded represents the amount of standard solution added to the sample.

The RRs and RSDs of the spiked samples were in the range of 75.6–103.9% and 4.3–10.3%, respectively. Based on these findings, the proposed method is accurate and sensitive enough for determination of the target analytes in real matrices. Fig. 5 displays the GC chromatograms of standard solution of target analytes, un-spiked, and spiked apple sample with two different concentrations after the extraction process under optimal conditions. Furthermore, Fig. S15–17 exhibit GC chromatograms of various real samples, including carrot, potato, and agricultural water, respectively.

Additionally, the matrix effect value (ME) was calculated using Eq: (6).

Where Rm and Rw are the recovery of the target analytes in the real sample and ultra-pure water, respectively. Based on the results shown in Table 2, It can be concluded that the matrix had no significant effect on the determination of the target analytes in the apple sample. In contrast, the determination of some target analytes in potato, carrot and agricultural water was influenced by the matrix.

Table 3 compares the analytical performance of the proposed method with previous studies that utilized novel sorbents for the extraction of pesticides and fungicides in various real samples. Sereshti et al. synthesized three-dimensional magnetic graphene coated with gold nanoparticles (3D-MG@AuNP), and utilized it for OPPs extraction from wastewater and tap water samples, prior to GC-μECD analysis. The proposed method exhibited a lower LOD for OPPs compared to our work. This discrepancy may be attributed to their utilization of GC-μECD, a more sensitive detection instrument for OPPs, in contrast to the instrument (GC-FID) employed in our study (Sereshti et al., 2023). Shakourian et al. synthesized a magnetic metal-organic framework composite (Fe3O4@TGA@TMU-6) and utilized it for extraction of OPPs from water and rice samples, before HPLC– UV analysis. Metal-organic framework (MOF) has a large surface area and porous structure; therefore, it showed an affinity for target analytes. This method showed higher PF and lower LOD than ours (Shakourian, Yamini, & Safari, 2020). In another study, magnetic multiwalled carbon nanotube-molecularly imprinted polymer (MMWCNT-MIP) was employed as a sorbent for OPPs (fenitrothion) determination before IMS analysis. The fabricated sorbent benefits from MMWCNT properties, including high chemical and mechanical resistance, large surface areas, and the convenience of separation using an external magnetic field, eliminating the need for extra centrifugation or filtration steps. This method displayed comparable results with our study (Khojasteh & Bazmandegan-Shamili, 2022). Wang et al. reported magnetic graphene nanocomposite (graphene@SiO2@Fe3O4) for extraction of OPPs from water, before GC-FPD analysis. The advantage of this work was the chemical bonding of graphene to Fe3O4@SiO2, where the stability of the material could potentially be enhanced through covalent bonding. Our work provided lower LODs than this work (P. Wang et al., 2018). In a recent study, Fe3O4 hollow microspheres based on a covalent organic framework (COF) were developed as an adsorbent for TFs extraction from food samples, prior to GC-FTD. The fabricated sorbent possesses a large specific surface area, numerous pores, and chemical stability, making it suitable as an adsorbent. This method demonstrated a shorter extraction time compared to our work, and it exhibited comparable values for RSD, LOD, and linearity (Y.-X. Wang, Shen, Feng, & Pang, 2023). Aladaghlo et al. reported an ultrasonic assisted dispersive solid phase microextraction method (UA-DSPME), utilizing a cellulose-based nanocomposite as a sorbent for extraction of TFs from real samples, before GC-FID analysis. In this work, carboxymethylcellulose was modified with graphene oxide and MOF. The extraction time in this method is very short, which may be attributed to the use of ultrasonication that enhances the contact between the target analytes and adsorptive sites of the adsorbent, resulting in an increase in mass transfer. Furthermore, this method provided better PF, comparable RSD and LOD values, and wider linearity than ours (Aladaghlo et al., 2023). Magnetic porous organic polymers with high surface area, good dispersibility, and stability were synthesized and employed for the extraction of TFs from vegetable samples. Analytical factors, such as linearity and RSDs, show similarities to those observed in our study. Overall, the achieved LODs and PF in this study are either comparable to or superior to those of other methods (Li, He, Chen, & Hu, 2019). However, the advantage of the proposed method lies in the utilization of a new magnetic carbon nanocomposite that can be synthesized in one step and is made from naturally available, low-cost, and environmentally friendly materials. In contrast, some previously reported studies have employed expensive and non-degradable materials and multiple steps for preparing the adsorbent.

The study focused on synthesizing a new green magnetic carbon nanocomposite via a straightforward one-step hydrothermal method and evaluating its efficacy as an adsorbent for extraction both TFs and OPPs. In this case, cheap, biocompatible, abundant, and renewable raw materials were used as principles of green chemistry synthesis. The magnetic carbon nanocomposite has surface functional groups, such as OH, NH, COOH, CC and CO that facilitated the adsorption of target analytes in different samples. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed MDSPE-GC-FID method, which is based on the magnetic carbon nanocomposite, demonstrated satisfactory linearity, recoveries, limits of detection (LODs), limits of quantification (LOQs), and relative standard deviations (RSDs). These results indicate that the method is effective and accurate in analyzing residues of TFs and OPPs in complex samples, making it a suitable choice for food safety analysis. In summary, the novelty of this work lies in the synthesis of a magnetic carbon nanocomposite using new materials as a sorbent for pesticide analysis. The results of this study demonstrate that the hydrothermal process can be effectively utilized to produce a new type of adsorbent for extraction and preconcentration fungicides and pesticides in complex sample matrices. However, the disadvantage of the hydrothermal process for synthesizing sorbents is the requirement for high temperatures and pressures. This may not be compatible with certain materials or may result in undesired chemical reactions or phase transformations, limiting the range of materials that can be synthesized using hydrothermal process. The disadvantage of the proposed method is that it utilizes a greater amount of sorbent and requires longer extraction times compared to other studies. Furthermore, using GC-FID as the detection instrument demonstrates lower sensitivity compared to other instruments such as GC–MS.
